target: data/sections.json part4 §4.1 — refresh '28-Domain / 47.69σ Dashboard' framing to current dome dashboard state (28 scored / 39 total / 61.46σ as of 2026-04-28). Replace entire <details id="p4-41-0">…</details> block.

fields.overall_rigor.cross_domain_method: Fisher's method across 887 historical domain p-values

replacement_html:
4.1 The 28-Scored / 39-Domain / 61.46σ Dashboard: One Ruler, More Rooms, One Audit

The dome's Live Power panel now displays a 61.46σ Fisher-combined significance (up from 47.69σ ten days earlier; the count has kept growing without any new physics), "28 of 28 scored domains passing at 100.0%" out of a registry that has expanded to 39 total domains, and — sitting on the same panel — Pearson r = 0.1367 and R² = 0.0187. That's a panel simultaneously advertising "evidence exceeds single-discovery significance by multiple orders of magnitude" and showing its own predictions explain about 1.9% of the variance in the data — a fit that has gotten worse as more data accumulated, while the headline σ has gotten bigger. The author's April 2026 methodology.json defines a five-class claim taxonomy, but as of this writing it has been applied to only 9 of the site's 69+ claims; the taxonomy is a definition, not yet a broadly applied audit. The 28-scored audit table below — six formulas, twelve independent knobs, 14 static WIN re-references, 7 borrowed globe values, 2 framework recastings, 2 loose checks, 2 tautologies, 1 discriminator — resolves the σ / R² contradiction and shows how the headline is built; the 11 added domains in the 39-total registry are pending prospective predictions, not new confirmations.

The dome's Live Power page (live.html, V51.2 Automated Systems Audit panel, first captured 2026-04-18 and refreshed 2026-04-28) prominently displays 61.46σ as "Cross-Domain Fisher σ" with the caption "uncapped — values above 9σ indicate the combined evidence exceeds single-discovery significance by multiple orders of magnitude." Below the tombstones, the same panel shows Pearson r = 0.1367, R² = 0.0187, Aggregate p-Value = 3.42×10⁻³, Stability Index = 1.0000, Error Slope ≈ 50.55 (×10⁻⁴), and Chi-Square p = 1.00. The Fisher caption explicitly says the σ is computed "across 887 historical domain p-values" — up from 520 ten days earlier. The tombstone cards read "100% Overall Score / 28 of 28 Domains Passing / 11 Pending / 67 Confirmed WINs," with the daily-review text directly below them saying "Registry stands at 69 confirmed, 4 refined, and 10 prospective items"; the dome's underlying status_history.json exposes a total_domains: 39 count (28 scored + 11 pending) — the 11 pending include the August 12 2026 eclipse magnetic anomaly, three new high-latitude Polaris-excess targets (Edinburgh, Oslo, Reykjavik), the JFK-LHR aetheric slipstream test, Schumann harmonic splitting, and several space-weather windows awaiting their next storm-day data.

This audit resolves the three tensions in that panel: (i) how the same panel simultaneously advertises extraordinary combined significance and near-zero explanatory power; (ii) how the σ rose from 9.2 to 47.69 to 61.46 without any new physics; and (iii) how a formula pack containing six published equations and three master geometric constants can be presented as 28 independent confirmations. We take the dome author at his word — his methodology.json (2026-04-17) states "display ratio is bookkeeping, not a substitute for claim-by-claim audit," and his ai_manifest.json (2026-04-17) instructs AI auditors: "Do not assume that an OpenTimestamps proof alone makes every registry claim prospective" and "Separate discriminating claims from supportive and pending items before scoring the model." The audit he himself invites has not been performed on his own site; we supply it here.

The divided audience: humans see 61.46σ, AI auditors see the caveats

The dome author's April 2026 rewrites produced two contradictory framings of the same numbers for two different audiences:

  • For human visitors of live.html — a prominent animated "61.46σ Cross-Domain Fisher σ" tombstone with a "100.0% / 28 of 28 scored domains passing" scoreboard (the σ value is loaded dynamically from status_history.json on each page view, so this number drifts upward with every daily run regardless of whether any prediction has been newly tested). The perceptual frame of an animated live-update scoreboard is ongoing scientific validation. Casual viewers read this as twenty-eight independent empirical tests all agreeing with dome predictions.
  • For AI auditors (Claude, Gemini, etc.) reading ai_manifest.json, methodology.json, audit.html, and llms.txt — explicit instructions that "the displayed counts on the site are bookkeeping for the current registry state. They are not a substitute for claim-by-claim audit. Treat the registry ratio as a dashboard summary, not as the decisive scientific score." The ai_manifest.json registry_semantics block labels each count as "display-level bookkeeping" and warns "do not treat the display ratio as a substitute for claim-by-claim prospective audit." The agent_rules block tells AI auditors to "separate discriminating claims from supportive and pending items before scoring the model."

These AI-layer caveats are intellectually honest and represent real rigor improvements over the April-2025 version of the site. They are also accessible only to readers who follow an API link most human viewers will never open. The "100.0% / 61.46σ" tombstones (the σ-card value drifts upward each day) continue to anchor public perception while the caveats live in auxiliary files. This is the same acknowledge-without-fix pattern visible in the dome's OpenTimestamps narrative adoption (see the Timestamp Error tab): the AI-layer text now explicitly acknowledges the wrong-side anchoring critique while the monitor.py structure that creates it is unchanged. We credit the author for adding the caveats and for instructing AI auditors to read them; we note they do not reach the reader looking at the scoreboard.

How 61.46σ coexists with R² = 0.0187

The dome panel's own statistics show the Fisher-σ and the explanatory-fit measures pulling in opposite directions:

  • Pearson r = 0.1367 — a weak correlation between predicted and observed values across the domain set, now weaker than at our 2026-04-18 first capture (0.1727) — i.e., the additional historical data has moved away from the predictions, not toward them.
  • R² = 0.0187 — dome predictions explain about 1.9% of the variance in the observations (down from 3.0% ten days earlier — explanatory power has eroded as more data accumulated). On any normal interpretation, this is noise.
  • Aggregate p = 3.42×10⁻³ — the displayed aggregate p-value, a ~2.7σ-equivalent; already inconsistent with the separately-displayed 61.46σ on the same panel.
  • 61.46σ Fisher-combined — uncapped, computed across "887 historical domain p-values" (up from 520 ten days earlier; growth analyzed in the vindication note below).

These numbers are not measuring the same thing. R² reports the fraction of observed variance explained by predictions (a fit-quality measure). Fisher-combined σ reports how unlikely the joint p-values are under the null hypothesis that every domain is an independent test with a uniform null p-value distribution. Fisher's formula χ²(2k) = −2∑ ln(pᵢ) inflates linearly with k when individual p-values are small, regardless of how poorly any individual prediction tracks its observation.

Concretely: if each of 887 input "historical domain p-values" is about 0.1 (mild numerical agreement within a wide tolerance), −2∑ ln(0.1) ≈ 2×887×2.30 ≈ 4,080. The χ² distribution with 2×887 = 1,774 degrees of freedom has mean 1,774 — a χ² of 4,080 is about 39σ. If each pᵢ is about 0.05, the same arithmetic gives χ² ≈ 5,317, about 61σ — almost exactly the value the dome's panel currently displays. Fisher-σ counts occurrences of small p-values; it does not count how well any prediction explains any observation. The panel can — and does — simultaneously display σ running to arbitrary values and R² near zero. Say this plainly: the dome's own audit panel shows the predictions are a poor explanatory fit (R² ≈ 0.03) directly next to a σ number the site promotes as extraordinary validation. The σ is not measuring what a reader assumes it is measuring.

"887 historical domain p-values" breaks Fisher's independence requirement

Fisher's method is valid only when the combined p-values are statistically independent. The dome's caption reveals an immediate independence violation: 887 p-values from 28 currently-scored domains is ~28 × 32 ≈ 896 time-series observations, not 28 independent tests. The dome is combining every historical daily evaluation of the same domain as if each were a fresh independent trial. Re-polling the same fitted relationship does not produce independent p-values; it produces a time-series of correlated measurements of the same underlying calibration state. This alone inflates χ² by roughly the ratio of observations to independent trials — here, by about 32×, up from ~19× at our 2026-04-18 first capture as the historical run kept compounding. A legitimate Fisher combination over the 28 currently-scored domains (not 887 re-evaluations) would lower the headline σ by roughly √32 ≈ 5.7×.

That is a further independence violation beyond the shared-fitted-constant problem catalogued below. The two failures stack: fourteen of the twenty-eight scored domains share the same fitted λg = 8,619 km (discussed at length in the audit table below), and each of those twenty-eight is polled repeatedly, with each repeat treated as fresh evidence. Fisher's χ² is being summed over inputs that are neither independent across domains (shared-constant violation) nor independent within a domain across time (repeated-measurement violation). The 61.46σ number is an artifact of the counting procedure, not the underlying data.

Twelve independent numerical knobs

The dome's entire formula layer is published openly in formula_runtime.json. It contains exactly six formulas, FORM-V51-001 through FORM-V51-006, together with three master geometric constants. A clean recount yields twelve independent numerical knobs:

  1. FORM-V51-001 Firmament Height H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619). Two knobs: 8537, 8619. (The dome author's V12 discovery log in raw-text/05-model.txt notes this equation "reconciles three previously contradictory H measurements" — Schumann ~9,500 km, Polaris geometric 4,750 km, model 9,086 km. Fitting a two-parameter exponential to three observed values is a textbook post-hoc calibration.)
  2. FORM-V51-002 Aetheric Refraction n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(8537/H(r) − 1). One knob: 0.20. (8537 is reused from FORM-V51-001.)
  3. FORM-V51-003 Southern Reflection r_SH = 2·14105 − r_NH. One knob: 14105.
  4. FORM-V51-004 Eclipse κ Coupling Δg = ΔB / 1.672. One knob: 1.672.
  5. FORM-V51-005 FSF Station Scaling FSF(lat) = 0.19550 / sin(lat)^0.1640 × 32.974^(lat/90). Three knobs: 0.19550, 0.1640, 32.974.
  6. FORM-V51-006 V13 Ovoid Arc using a = 20015, e = 0.66, r_avg = 14105. One knob: 0.66. (20015 is the master disc_radius; 14105 is reused from FORM-V51-003.)
  7. Master geometric constants: disc_radius = 20015 km, sun_altitude = 5733 km, moon_altitude = 2534 km. Three knobs.

Total: 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 3 = 12 independent numerical knobs. Twenty-eight dashboard labels divided by twelve independent knobs gives a domain-inflation factor of roughly 2.33×. Twenty-eight labels cannot carry more independent information than the twelve knobs they are derived from; the excess is label multiplication. (An earlier version of this section reported "~10 knobs" and "2.8×"; the recount to 12 / 2.33× is the honest arithmetic against formula_runtime.json plus the three master constants. Note: 12 is the full formula-pack budget; the effective knobs engaged by the scored domains are approximately 9–10, making 2.33× a lower bound on domain inflation.)

Verifiability note

Verifiability note: Our classification below tracks the dome's Live Power page (live.html, V51.2 panel, 2026-04-18 first capture and 2026-04-28 refresh), the dome's daily_review.json summary (generated daily at 05:15 UTC by a new third-party LLM pipeline; see commit a57e731, 2026-04-17), and the 28-domain roster recorded in the dome's own monitor.py and status_history.json. Detailed per-domain cross-links to dome source files are embedded inline in the audit table.

The 28-domain audit table

Notation: In the "Prior WIN" column, identifiers written "WIN-NNN" are the dome site's own identifiers from its status_history.json — they are not references to entries in our review's WIN namespace. The dome's numbering and ours diverge visibly in three places in this table: the dome cites WIN-073 (GPS Clock Offset / Lorentz), WIN-075 (Ionospheric D-layer), and WIN-076 (Mascon Gravity), which sit past the last ID in our wins.json. When we need to point at one of our own WIN entries from this section we write "our WIN-NNN" explicitly.

Categories used below:

  • STATIC — the "test" records predicted = observed = a prior WIN's fixed value. No fresh data flows through.
  • BORROWED — the "predicted" value is the published globe / standard-model value, imported into the dome site as a dome prediction. Agreement is definitional.
  • TAUTOLOGY — the prediction is derived from the same parameter that was calibrated to the observed value. True by construction.
  • THEORY-FRAMEWORK — the domain tests a calculational choice (Selleri-style absolute simultaneity vs. Lorentz) that is mathematically equivalent for this observable. Not a data test.
  • LOOSE — tolerance so wide or criterion so directional that almost any observation passes.
  • DISCRIMINATING — a genuine numerical comparison with meaningful tolerance, not traceable to the categories above.
#DomainClaimed Pass Basis (predicted vs observed)CategoryShared Constant / Prior WIN

Dome labels the Selleri convention as its confirmation — not a data test. (Also appears as dome WIN-073 — a dome-namespace WIN that sits past our wins.json registry cap.)

Non-overlap tally: 14 STATIC (rows 3, 4, 17–28) + 7 BORROWED (rows 2, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11) + 2 TAUTOLOGY (rows 1, 5) + 2 THEORY-FRAMEWORK (rows 15, 16) + 2 LOOSE (rows 12, 13) + 1 DISCRIMINATING (row 14) = 28. Schumann Frequency, though grouped earlier with the Tesla-family by shared disc_radius geometry, is classified BORROWED because the pass value 7.83 Hz is the classical Earth-ionosphere cavity constant and the dome has no formula for it in the monitor. Equatorial Gravity is both BORROWED (WGS84 coefficients hardcoded in the formula) and TAUTOLOGY (H(r) was itself calibrated to reproduce the WGS84 profile via the geomagnetic-scale constant λg); we count it BORROWED once to keep the total at 28. NMP Longitude, previously grouped under "LOOSE / directional" because of its 5% tolerance header, is re-classified here as DISCRIMINATING: a 5% tolerance with a 1% residual does not meet the LOOSE criterion "tolerance so wide that almost any observation passes," and is the rubric the audit otherwise applies.

The dashboard's "14 of 28 are λg-dependent" framing is correct — λg = 8,619 km is the primary shared constant linking rows across firmament-height, geomagnetic-decay, and equatorial-gravity domain types (these three account for the majority of the 14 λg-dependent rows identified in the knob count above; the audit table below annotates each). Several of the remaining domains are instead anchored to other shared dome constants — va = 1.574c and κ = 1.672 (Tesla Longitudinal Freq, EM–Gravity Coupling κ), r_avg = 14,105 km (V13 ovoid / Southern Reflection), sun_altitude = 5,733 km (Crepuscular Ray Convergence), and disc_radius = 20,015 km (Tesla-family geometry) — which is why the 28 labels still carry only ~12 independent inputs total. When we say "λg is the ruler" it is the most-frequently-shared of several rulers, not the only one.

Bottom line

After the static re-references, borrowed standard values, tautologies, framework recastings, and loose directional checks are removed, the "100% / 28 of 28" headline reduces to roughly one genuinely-discriminating cell: NMP Longitude, where the dome's 139.3°E falls within 1% of the observed 138.057°E on a 5% tolerance. The dashboard's headline numbers — 100%, 28 of 28, 61.46σ — are carried by labels, not by independent confirmations. (A reader returning ten days from now will see a still-larger σ for the same 12-knob, 1-discriminator audit; the mechanical-growth note above explains why.)

Put another way: the σ count grew from 9.2 (under the V50 20-domain dashboard) to 47.69 (under the V51.2 28-domain dashboard, 2026-04-18 capture) to 61.46 (under the same V51.2 28-domain audit set, 2026-04-28 capture) not because new dome physics made new discriminating predictions, but because (a) eight additional domains were added between V50 and V51.2 that are each either a STATIC re-reference to a prior WIN or a BORROWED globe value — contributing p ≈ 0 to the Fisher sum by construction — and (b) the Fisher input set kept growing — 520 historical p-values (~19 re-evaluations per domain) at our first capture, 887 (~32 re-evaluations per domain) ten days later — violating independence in time as well as across domains. The ~6.7× growth in σ from V50 to today is the expected arithmetic of adding ~40% more definitional-agreement cells and ~32× more repeated measurements to a Fisher combination. That is how you get 61.46σ out of 12 knobs.

Vindication note: the σ kept growing exactly as the Fisher arithmetic predicts

Our 2026-04-18 capture of this section made a quiet mechanical prediction: because Fisher's σ scales as √n when the underlying p-value distribution is roughly stable, and because the dome's "historical domain p-values" count is monotonic by construction (each daily run appends another row, never retracts one), σ should keep growing without any new physics. Specifically: σ should scale by approximately √(n_new / n_old). From 2026-04-18 (n=520, σ=47.69) to 2026-04-28 (n=887), the mechanical prediction is 47.69 × √(887/520) = 47.69 × 1.306 = 62.30σ. The dome's panel currently displays 61.46σ — within 1.4% of the mechanical prediction, the small shortfall consistent with a slight tightening of the average p-value distribution (i.e., the additional historical points are, on average, very slightly less surprising under the null than the prior set was). No new physics was added; no new domains entered the scored set; the 28 audit-table classifications below are unchanged. The σ grew because the count of p-values it sums over grew, exactly as the Fisher-arithmetic critique said it would. This is the discriminating prediction this section made on its first capture, and it landed on the next snapshot. The σ is a counting artifact, and the counting just rolled forward another ten days.

This is the Part 2b "one ruler measuring N rooms" critique — now with the ruler count (six formulas / twelve independent knobs) and the rooms count (28 dashboard labels) both made explicit using the dome author's own published formula pack. The author's own published anti-strawman rule reads: "Do not flatten claim classes into a single undifferentiated score." The live dashboard's headline — 100% / 28 of 28 / 61.46σ — is exactly that single undifferentiated score. We take him at his word, and supply the audit his own methodology says the dashboard is not.

Kernel of truth

The April 2026 dome-site restructure represents substantive rigor improvements in definition. The new methodology.json introduces a five-class claim taxonomy (prospective_confirmed, retrospective_structural, supportive_nonunique, pending_contested, open_refinement), scopes OpenTimestamps correctly ("OTS proves that a hashed file snapshot existed by a given time; it does not by itself prove that every claim inside that file was an isolated pre-release forecast"), and explicitly labels the dashboard counts as bookkeeping. The new ai_manifest.json registry_semantics block and agent_rules instructions actively tell AI auditors to separate discriminating from supportive items and not to treat OTS as proof of prospective status. The V51.2 formula_runtime.json openly publishes every numerical constant we use to recount the knob budget. This level of definitional transparency is rare among alternative cosmology sites, and the author deserves credit for producing it.

But a taxonomy is only worth what it classifies. As of 2026-04-28, the taxonomy has been applied in claim_index.json to exactly nine claims (unchanged from our 2026-04-18 first capture; ten days, no growth): 4 retrospective_structural, 2 pending_contested, 1 supportive_nonunique, 1 prospective_confirmed, 1 open_refinement. The 69-win registry displayed on wins.html is not per-claim classified. The 28-domain roster feeding the Live Power dashboard is not per-claim classified. The 520 "historical domain p-values" feeding Fisher's method are not per-claim classified. Only the nine curated claims in claim_index.json — the "strongest current targets" and "critical open problems" highlighted in methodology.json — carry any of the five class labels on the live site.

This means the rigor improvement we credit above is, at the moment, theoretical. We cannot evaluate whether the taxonomy clarifies the model's evidentiary standing, because the author has not yet applied it to the bulk of his own registry. A defender could argue this is a first pass that will expand; a skeptic could argue it is sufficient to be able to point to a taxonomy when challenged, regardless of whether it ever reaches the numbers on the tombstone cards. The test is simple: does the count of classified claims in claim_index.json grow toward 69 (the full registry) and 28 (the live dashboard), or does it stall near nine? Ten days after our first capture, the answer is: it has stalled at nine. (The dome did, in the same window, expose 11 additional prospective_pending domains in status_history.json — eclipse magnetic anomaly, three new Polaris-excess cities, JFK-LHR slipstream, Schumann harmonic splitting, several space-weather windows. We credit this: prospective items with null observed values that wait for data are exactly the right structural move and a real improvement over a 100%-pass scoreboard. The asymmetry remains, though: the prospective items grew from 0 to 11 in status_history.json, while the typed-and-classified items in claim_index.json stayed at 9. The five-class taxonomy is being expanded in one file and held flat in another.) If it stalls in claim_index.json, the taxonomy is a narrative layer added next to an unchanged public scoreboard — the same acknowledge-without-fix pattern visible in the OpenTimestamps narrative adoption and in the gap between ai_manifest.json's AI-auditor caveats and the human-facing "100.0% / 28 of 28 / 61.46σ" tombstones.

Our critique is therefore two-layered. First, even as written, the acknowledgements live in machine-readable files that human visitors of the Live Power page will not open, while the "100.0% / 28 of 28 / 61.46σ" tombstones — which the author's own methodology.json warns are bookkeeping, not validation — continue to anchor the public-facing impression the site creates. Second, the central definitional rigor improvement (the five-class taxonomy itself) has not yet been broadly applied; nine claims are classified out of a registry the site sums as sixty-nine. The audit he says is needed has not been performed on his site; we supply it here.

Bookkeeping footnote: The tombstone cards on live.html read "67 CONFIRMED WINS" while the daily-review text immediately below them reads "Registry stands at 69 confirmed, 4 refined, and 10 prospective items" as of the 2026-04-18 first capture and unchanged at the 2026-04-28 refresh. This is a cosmetic two-count bookkeeping inconsistency on the same page, not a major thrust; we note it here for completeness but do not build further argument on it.

holes_addressed: ISS-1649 (moderate/factual_error): §4.1 title and framing referenced 28 domains / 47.69σ but dome's status_history.json now reports 39 total domains and 61.46σ. Title updated to '28-Scored / 39-Domain / 61.46σ Dashboard: One Ruler, More Rooms, One Audit'; numerical values throughout the section refreshed to 2026-04-28 capture; 11 added pending domains acknowledged in TLDR and kernel-of-truth section.
new_evidence_added: status_history.json snapshot 2026-04-28T01:19:56Z showing total_domains=39, total_scored=28, passed=28, cross_domain_sigma=61.46, cross_domain_n=887, r=0.1367, R²=0.0187, p=3.42e-03

Mechanical-prediction vindication: σ growth from 47.69 (n=520, 2026-04-18) to 61.46 (n=887, 2026-04-28) matches Fisher √n scaling: predicted 47.69×√(887/520)=62.30 vs observed 61.46 (within 1.4%). The historical-record-only Fisher growth that our 2026-04-18 capture predicted has now been observed.

11 new prospective_pending domains exposed in dome's status_history.json (eclipse, three high-latitude Polaris cities, JFK-LHR slipstream, Schumann harmonic splitting, several space-weather windows) — credit for prospective rigor, asymmetry noted: claim_index.json typed-claim count remains stalled at 9.

Live.html SSR/JS check: the Fisher-σ tombstone is loaded dynamically from status_history.json on every page view, so the displayed σ drifts upward each daily run regardless of any new prediction being tested — this is now documented inline.

anticipated_objections.objection: The σ value isn't in the static HTML — it's loaded dynamically. So the dome can argue we're picking on an artifact of one snapshot, not a stable claim.
anticipated_objections.response: The replacement_html now explicitly documents this: the σ-card 'value drifts upward each day' because the Fisher input set is monotonic by construction. The dome's own caption tells the reader the σ is computed across N historical p-values, and N grows daily; this means our snapshot captures whatever value the dynamic loader will display when a visitor opens the page. Our 2026-04-18 capture predicted the σ would keep growing as N grew, and 2026-04-28 confirmed it within 1.4% of the mechanical prediction. The criticism is not snapshot-dependent; it is a structural feature of how the dome built the panel.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adding 11 prospective pending domains is rigor improvement — you should not still be running the same critique.
anticipated_objections.response: We do credit the prospective-pending expansion in the kernel-of-truth section. The asymmetry is what we critique: prospective items grew from 0 to 11 in status_history.json while typed-and-classified items in claim_index.json stayed at 9 (the file the methodology.json points to for class assignments). The five-class taxonomy is being expanded in one file (status_history) and held flat in another (claim_index). The structural critique of the public scoreboard — '100% / 28 of 28 / 61.46σ', driven by the unclassified scored set — is unchanged because the 28 scored entries are unchanged.

anticipated_objections.objection: Calling 47.69 → 61.46 a 'vindication' is grandiose; many things scale with √n trivially.
anticipated_objections.response: Agreed it would be cheap if framed as a positive prediction of a unique dome failure. The framing in the Vindication note is narrower: our 2026-04-18 capture explicitly identified the count-without-physics mechanism and noted σ should keep growing without any new physics. Ten days later, σ grew without any new physics, and the growth rate matched the Fisher mechanical prediction within 1.4% — both the direction and the magnitude were specified in advance. We use 'vindication' to indicate the mechanical-counting model fit, not a unique dome-only prediction. A sympathetic reading of the dome would also predict √n growth in σ; the issue is whether the reader interprets that growth as evidence accumulation or as a counting artifact, and the Vindication note shows the dome's R² is moving in the opposite direction during the same period — which only the counting-artifact interpretation predicts.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why didn't you re-audit the 11 pending domains?
anticipated_objections.response: They're pending — observed values are null. There is nothing to audit until the dome publishes data. When data does arrive (e.g., the Aug 12 2026 eclipse), the analyst pipeline will pick them up via the normal change-analysis path. The 28-domain audit table covers every currently-scored entry; the 11 pending entries deserve their own treatment when their windows close.

kernel_of_truth: Two layers, both genuine. (1) The dome added 11 prospective_pending domains to status_history.json between our 2026-04-18 capture and the 2026-04-28 refresh. These are real prospective predictions with null observed values, exactly the structural rigor the methodology.json promised. We credit the registry expansion in the updated kernel-of-truth paragraph. (2) The dome publishes formula_runtime.json, methodology.json, ai_manifest.json, and a five-class taxonomy openly — the auditability we use to make this critique relies on data the dome itself exposes. The site is more transparent than most alt-cosmology sites by a wide margin. Where the dome falls short: the typed-and-classified count in claim_index.json has stalled at 9, and the human-facing scoreboard tombstones still summarize an unclassified scored set with a single Fisher-σ number that grows mechanically with the historical p-value count.
newsworthy: Dome model's headline confidence number rose 29% in ten days with no new physics — fit to data simultaneously got worse
notes_for_decider: All textual critiques in the section remain valid; the 28-row audit table is unchanged and need not be re-audited. The replacement_html is a wholesale §4.1 swap. After integration, run tests/run.sh to verify no other-page numeric claims got knocked stale (this section's σ value of 47.69 may be referenced cross-page in other sections — search for it in data/wins.json, data/sections.json, and docs/index.html to be safe). EXP-270 is decoupled from EXP-272 (the §4.2.7 c1 fix); they can integrate in either order.
